calendar.matters:
Notice Of Motion For Order Permitting Plaintiff To File An Application For Renewal Of Judgment, Nunc Pro Tunc On July 14, 2020; Memorandum Of Points And Authorities; Declaration Of William G Mcdevitt; Proposed Order
Off Calendar Sep-17-2020 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES:

HEARING RE: PLAINTIFF ARMAND RIOS's Motion For Order Permitting Plaintiff To File An Application For Renewal Of Judgment, Nunc Pro Tunc On July 14, 2020.

No appearances made in this matter. The Court adopts its tentative ruling.

Plaintiff's motion for an order permitting him to file an application for renewal of judgment nunc pro tunc on July 14, 2020 is denied. On or about November 17, 1989-31 years ago-plaintiff obtained judgment against defendant in the amount of $250,285.35. That judgment was renewed on or about October 20, 1995, a second time on or about September 12, 2002, and a third time on July 14, 2010. As of July 14, 2010, with accrued interest, the original amount of the judgment had ballooned to $1,193,083.63. Plaintiff makes no showing that he has ever sought to collect on the judgment.

On July 21, 2020, more than ten years after the last renewal, plaintiff submitted a fourth request to renew the judgment, which the Clerk rejected on the ground that the ten-year period to renew the judgment had expired on July 14, 2020. (Code Civ. Proc. sec. 683.020.) Generally, a money judgment is enforceable for 10 years after entry, but the enforcement period may be extended by renewal of the judgment. (Code Civ. Proc. secs. 683.020, 683.120(a).) However, the application must be filed "at any time before the expiration of the 10-year period of enforceability provided by Section 683.020." (Code Civ. Proc. sec. 683.130(a).) That ten-year period runs from "the date of entry" of the judgment, not from the date on which the judgment creditor filed the notice of renewal of judgment. (Code Civ. Proc. sec. 680.020.) The Code provides that "[e]xcept as otherwise provided by statute, upon the expiration of 10 years after the date of entry of a money judgment . . . (a) The judgment may not be enforced," "(b) All enforcement procedures pursuant to the judgment or to a writ or order issued pursuant to the judgment shall cease," and "(c) Any lien created by an enforcement procedure pursuant to the judgment is extinguished." (Code Civ. Proc. sec. 683.020.)

Plaintiff does not cite any authority that supports his contention that the court's "inherent power" authorizes it to enter an order that would render timely an untimely filed application for renewal. A court's inherent power to enter a nunc pro tunc order extends only "'to show that a thing was done at one time which ought to have been shown at that time. It is an entry now for something previously done so that the record may actually speak the truth.'" (Martin v. Martin (1970) 2 Cal.3d 752, 760-761.) Plaintiff does not show that he timely filed, or even sought to file, the renewal application. His attempt to blame the COVID-19 crisis or the court's e-filing system for his own delay is unavailing. The court has accepted e-filings throughout the past several months, notwithstanding the partial shutdown of operations due to COVID-19 and deemed court holidays, which ended on June 1, 2020. Further, the record reflects that plaintiff's counsel did not request the Clerk's office to e-file his application until July 20, 2020, which was after the expiration of the ten-year period, and that the Clerk's office responded within a single day. (McDevitt Decl., Ex. E.) Accordingly, the motion must be denied. (See Hamilton v. Laine (1997) 57 Cal.App.4th 885 [order nunc pro tunc exceeded the limited permissible purpose, and therefore was erroneously entered].)

Order signed.

Judge: Ethan P. Schulman; Clerk: Sean Kane; Not Reported (302/EPS)
